content.text: Azure Professional Direct (ProDirect) support costs $1,000 USD per month per subscription. It extends Standard Support by adding a Designated Support Engineer (DSE), proactive advisory services, architecture reviews, and faster Severity B response times. It is aimed at organisations running enterprise workloads where architectural guidance and a named Microsoft contact add measurable value.

attrs.alt: Professional Direct vs Standard — unique capabilities and price comparison

content.text: What Professional Direct Adds Over Standard

The DSE is a named Microsoft engineer assigned to your subscription. They develop familiarity with your architecture, attend scheduled reviews, and are the escalation point for reactive cases. The DSE is not available 24x7 for reactive incidents — reactive cases still go through the standard 24x7 queue — but they accelerate resolution by already knowing your environment.
Scheduled monthly or quarterly architecture review sessions (30–60 min each).
Named point of contact for escalating cases that are stalling in the queue.
Proactive outreach when Microsoft identifies configuration risks in your services.
Access to preview documentation and early-access guidance for upcoming Azure features.
Can review Terraform/Bicep templates, pipeline design, or network topology before deployment.
The DSE relationship is advisory, not a guaranteed SLA. For reactive Severity A cases, the 24x7 response queue is still the primary channel regardless of DSE availability.

An architecture review is a structured session where the DSE and, optionally, additional Azure specialists evaluate your deployment against Azure Well-Architected Framework pillars: reliability, security, cost optimisation, operational excellence, and performance efficiency.

Export your resource inventory and network topology before the session so the DSE has concrete artefacts to review. The following script generates a structured inventory of your subscription resources grouped by type.

import json, requests
from collections import defaultdict
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

def export_resource_inventory(subscription_id: str) -> dict:
    """
    Export all resources in a subscription grouped by resource type.
    Use as input for a ProDirect architecture review session.
    """
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
        f"/resources?api-version=2021-04-01&$top=1000"
    )
    by_type = defaultdict(list)
    while url:
        resp = requests.get(url, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}, timeout=30)
        resp.raise_for_status()
        data = resp.json()
        for res in data.get("value", []):
            by_type[res["type"]].append({
                "name": res["name"],
                "location": res["location"],
                "resource_group": res["id"].split("/")[4],
                "sku": res.get("sku", {}).get("name"),
            })
        url = data.get("nextLink")

    return {
        "subscription_id": subscription_id,
        "resource_count": sum(len(v) for v in by_type.values()),
        "by_type": dict(sorted(by_type.items())),
    }

inventory = export_resource_inventory("your-subscription-id")
print(f"Total resources: {inventory['resource_count']}")
for rtype, resources in inventory["by_type"].items():
    print(f"  {rtype}: {len(resources)}")

with open("/tmp/inventory.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump(inventory, f, indent=2)
print("Inventory saved to /tmp/inventory.json — share with DSE before the session.")

# Generate an Azure Advisor score across all pillars — a quick WAF proxy
az advisor score list --output table

# Get high-impact reliability recommendations (key input for architecture review)
az advisor recommendation list \
  --filter "category eq 'HighAvailability'" \
  --query "[?impact=='High'].{resource:resourceMetadata.resourceId, title:shortDescription.problem}" \
  --output table

# Export Advisor findings to JSON for DSE review
az advisor recommendation list --output json > /tmp/advisor-recommendations.json
echo "Exported $(jq length /tmp/advisor-recommendations.json) recommendations"

# Security Centre secure score (another WAF pillar input)
az security secure-score list --output table
Operations Reviews: Tracking Improvements Over Time

Operations reviews (typically quarterly) track whether recommendations from architecture sessions have been implemented and measure the resulting improvement in Well-Architected scores. The following pattern generates a before/after comparison from Advisor score history.

import requests, datetime
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

def get_advisor_score_history(
    subscription_id: str,
    days: int = 90,
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Query Advisor score history for a subscription.
    Use to plot improvement between operations review cycles.
    """
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token

    end = datetime.datetime.utcnow()
    start = end - datetime.timedelta(days=days)

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.Advisor/advisorScore"
        f"?api-version=2023-01-01"
    )
    resp = requests.get(url, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}, timeout=30)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    scores = resp.json().get("value", [])

    result = []
    for s in scores:
        props = s.get("properties", {})
        result.append({
            "pillar": s["name"],
            "current_score": props.get("score"),
            "max_score": props.get("potentialScoreIncrease"),
            "last_refreshed": props.get("lastRefreshedScore"),
        })
    return result

scores = get_advisor_score_history("your-subscription-id")
print("Current Well-Architected scores:")
for s in scores:
    print(f"  {s['pillar']:30s}: {s['current_score']:.1f}/100 (potential +{s['max_score']:.1f})")
Priority Case Routing Under Professional Direct

ProDirect subscriptions have their cases prioritised in the support queue. When you create a case on a ProDirect subscription, Azure routes it to senior engineers with faster assignment. The case creation API is identical to Standard — the subscription's plan determines queue priority automatically.

import requests, os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

def create_prodirect_case(
    subscription_id: str,
    title: str,
    description: str,
    severity: str,
    service_id: str,
    problem_class_id: str,
    contact_email: str,
) -> dict:
    """
    Create a support case on a ProDirect subscription.
    No API difference from Standard — plan tier is inferred from the subscription.
    """
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token

    import re, datetime
    safe = re.sub(r"[^a-z0-9-]", "-", title.lower())[:40]
    ticket_name = f"{safe}-{datetime.datetime.utcnow().strftime('%Y%m%d%H%M')}"

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/supportTickets/{ticket_name}"
        "?api-version=2020-04-01"
    )
    body = {
        "properties": {
            "title": title,
            "description": description,
            "severity": severity,  # "critical" | "moderate" | "minimal"
            "serviceId": f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/services/{service_id}",
            "problemClassificationId": (
                f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/services/{service_id}"
                f"/problemClassifications/{problem_class_id}"
            ),
            "contactDetails": {
                "firstName": "Enterprise",
                "lastName": "Support",
                "primaryEmailAddress": contact_email,
                "preferredContactMethod": "email",
                "preferredSupportLanguage": "en-US",
                "country": "US",
                "preferredTimeZone": "UTC",
            },
        }
    }
    resp = requests.put(url, json=body,
                        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}, timeout=30)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    data = resp.json()
    return {
        "ticket_name": data["name"],
        "support_ticket_id": data["properties"].get("supportTicketId"),
        "status": data["properties"]["status"],
        "severity": data["properties"]["severity"],
    }

result = create_prodirect_case(
    subscription_id="your-prodirect-sub",
    title="AKS node pool autoscaler not scaling down after traffic drop",
    description=(
        "Standard_D4s_v3 node pool has not scaled below 8 nodes for 6 hours "
        "despite CPU < 10%. Cluster Autoscaler logs show no scale-down events. "
        "Production environment, 40 microservices affected."
    ),
    severity="moderate",
    service_id="<aks-service-id>",
    problem_class_id="<autoscaling-class-id>",
    contact_email="platform-team@company.com",
)
print(f"Ticket: {result['ticket_name']} — {result['status']}")
Is Professional Direct Worth the Cost?
The $1,000/month price is 10x Standard Support. The value depends on how you use the proactive capabilities — the reactive SLA improvement (Sev B 4 hrs → 2 hrs) alone rarely justifies the cost.
Faster Sev B response (< 2 hrs vs < 4 hrs)
Saves 2 hrs per moderate incident; meaningful only if you have frequent Sev B events
Equivalent to ~4 days of external Azure consulting per year at typical rates
DSE prevents costly design mistakes pre-production
One avoided re-architecture can exceed the annual plan cost ($12k) easily
Priority case routing for complex issues
Senior engineer assignment reduces back-and-forth by 1–2 days on complex cases
Operations reviews track WAF score improvements
Structures continuous improvement; difficult to quantify directly

Rule of thumb: if your team spends more than 10 hours per month waiting for Standard Support responses or does not have in-house Azure architecture expertise, Professional Direct typically pays for itself.
Setting Up a ProDirect Engagement Cadence

Getting value from Professional Direct requires deliberate scheduling. Microsoft will assign a DSE within a few weeks of plan activation, after which you should establish a regular cadence of sessions.
Architecture review — reliability and security pillars
Advisor recommendations export, current RBAC model
Operations review — WAF score progress, open issues
Architecture deep-dive on next quarter's initiatives

"""
Collect all data needed for a ProDirect architecture review session.
Outputs a ZIP of JSON files to share with the DSE before the meeting.
"""
import json, zipfile, subprocess, requests, datetime, os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

SUBSCRIPTION_ID = os.environ["AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID"]
OUT_DIR = "/tmp/prodirect-review"
os.makedirs(OUT_DIR, exist_ok=True)

def az(cmd: str) -> str:
    r = subprocess.run(f"az {cmd}", shell=True, capture_output=True, text=True)
    return r.stdout.strip()

def save(name: str, data) -> None:
    path = f"{OUT_DIR}/{name}.json"
    with open(path, "w") as f:
        json.dump(data if not isinstance(data, str) else {"raw": data}, f, indent=2)
    print(f"  Saved {name}.json")

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

print("Collecting review data...")

# 1. Resource inventory
url = f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{SUBSCRIPTION_ID}/resources?api-version=2021-04-01&$top=1000"
resp = requests.get(url, headers=headers, timeout=30)
resp.raise_for_status()
save("resource_inventory", resp.json())

# 2. Advisor recommendations
adv = az(f"advisor recommendation list --subscription {SUBSCRIPTION_ID} --output json")
save("advisor_recommendations", json.loads(adv) if adv.startswith("[") else {"raw": adv})

# 3. Advisor scores
scores = az(f"advisor score list --subscription {SUBSCRIPTION_ID} --output json")
save("advisor_scores", json.loads(scores) if scores.startswith("[") else {"raw": scores})

# 4. Role assignments
roles = az(f"role assignment list --subscription {SUBSCRIPTION_ID} --output json")
save("role_assignments", json.loads(roles) if roles.startswith("[") else {"raw": roles})

# 5. Policy compliance summary
policy_url = (
    f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{SUBSCRIPTION_ID}"
    f"/providers/Microsoft.PolicyInsights/policyStates/latest/summarize"
    "?api-version=2019-10-01"
)
policy_resp = requests.post(policy_url, headers=headers, timeout=30)
if policy_resp.ok:
    save("policy_compliance", policy_resp.json())

# 6. Security Centre secure score
sec = az(f"security secure-score list --subscription {SUBSCRIPTION_ID} --output json")
save("security_score", json.loads(sec) if sec.startswith("[") else {"raw": sec})

# Bundle into ZIP
zip_path = f"/tmp/prodirect-review-{datetime.datetime.utcnow().strftime('%Y%m%d')}.zip"
with zipfile.ZipFile(zip_path, "w", zipfile.ZIP_DEFLATED) as zf:
    for fname in os.listdir(OUT_DIR):
        zf.write(f"{OUT_DIR}/{fname}", fname)

print(f"\nReview package: {zip_path}")
print("Share this ZIP with your DSE at least 48 hours before the session.")

Architecture review sessions produce a list of recommendations. Tracking their implementation status in a structured file lets you report progress at the next operations review and demonstrates ROI from the plan.

import json, datetime

# recommendation_tracker.json — checked in to your ops repository
TRACKER_PATH = "docs/prodirect/recommendations.json"

def load_tracker() -> list[dict]:
    try:
        with open(TRACKER_PATH) as f:
            return json.load(f)
    except FileNotFoundError:
        return []

def add_recommendation(
    title: str,
    pillar: str,           # reliability | security | cost | performance | operations
    priority: str,         # high | medium | low
    description: str,
    owner: str,
    due_date: str,         # ISO date string
) -> dict:
    tracker = load_tracker()
    rec = {
        "id": f"rec-{len(tracker) + 1:03d}",
        "title": title,
        "pillar": pillar,
        "priority": priority,
        "description": description,
        "owner": owner,
        "due_date": due_date,
        "status": "open",            # open | in_progress | completed | deferred
        "created_at": datetime.date.today().isoformat(),
        "completed_at": None,
        "notes": [],
    }
    tracker.append(rec)
    with open(TRACKER_PATH, "w") as f:
        json.dump(tracker, f, indent=2)
    return rec

def update_status(rec_id: str, new_status: str, note: str = "") -> None:
    tracker = load_tracker()
    for rec in tracker:
        if rec["id"] == rec_id:
            rec["status"] = new_status
            if new_status == "completed":
                rec["completed_at"] = datetime.date.today().isoformat()
            if note:
                rec["notes"].append({"date": datetime.date.today().isoformat(), "text": note})
    with open(TRACKER_PATH, "w") as f:
        json.dump(tracker, f, indent=2)

def ops_review_summary() -> None:
    """Print a summary suitable for the quarterly operations review."""
    tracker = load_tracker()
    by_status = {}
    for rec in tracker:
        by_status.setdefault(rec["status"], []).append(rec)

    print("== ProDirect Operations Review Summary ==")
    for status, recs in sorted(by_status.items()):
        print(f"\n{status.upper()} ({len(recs)}):")
        for r in recs:
            print(f"  [{r['priority'].upper()}] {r['id']} — {r['title']} (owner: {r['owner']})")

# Example usage
add_recommendation(
    title="Enable zone-redundant deployment for App Service plan",
    pillar="reliability",
    priority="high",
    description="Current Standard S1 plan is single-zone. Migrate to Premium P1v3 with zone redundancy.",
    owner="platform-team",
    due_date="2026-08-01",
)
ops_review_summary()
Comparing ProDirect to Alternative Investment Strategies
Before purchasing Professional Direct, teams sometimes consider alternative ways to spend $12,000/year on Azure expertise. The trade-offs depend on the organisation's maturity and support frequency.
No advisory, no DSE, slower Sev B
Full managed ops or deep migration
Activating Professional Direct and Assigning the DSE

Professional Direct is purchased per subscription through the Azure portal. After activation, Microsoft assigns a DSE within 10–15 business days and sends an introduction email. The first step is scheduling the onboarding call.

# Verify your subscription has ProDirect active
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/providers/Microsoft.Support/supportPlanTypes?api-version=2021-06-01-preview" \
  --query "value[].{name:name, type:properties.planType}" \
  --output table

# List recent support tickets to confirm ProDirect routing
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/providers/Microsoft.Support/supportTickets?api-version=2020-04-01&\$top=5" \
  --query "value[].{id:name, severity:properties.severity, status:properties.status, engineer:properties.supportEngineer.emailAddress}" \
  --output table

# If no DSE assigned after 15 business days, escalate via a Severity C support case
# Title: "ProDirect DSE assignment not received — subscription {sub-id}"

Azure Professional Direct at $1,000/month per subscription adds a Designated Support Engineer, proactive advisory services, architecture reviews, faster Severity B response (< 2 hrs), and priority case routing on top of everything in Standard Support.
The DSE is a named Microsoft contact who learns your architecture and accelerates case escalation — not a 24x7 on-call resource.
Proactive advisory sessions (architecture reviews, operations reviews) are the core differentiator — reactive case handling is similar to Standard.
Resource inventory exports and Advisor score reports make architecture review sessions concrete and productive.
Case creation API is identical to Standard — ProDirect subscription tier determines queue priority automatically.
Justified when teams lack in-house Azure architecture depth or have frequent moderate-severity incidents where 2-hour vs 4-hour response makes a measurable difference.
